narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background includes upper-middle family income, full-time student status, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00—which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 30.50, notably lower than the average of 36.70. Your Year 1 school decile was 5.00, close to the average of 5.71, while your Year 3 decile was also 5.00, slightly under the average of 5.53. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours, estimated a 50.9% chance you'll fail the bar—tipping just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision hinged on just a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence on the prediction. Most notably, your Year 3 school decile ranking worked strongly in your favor, pushing the model toward expecting bar exam success because it suggested solid performance relative to peers. However, your LSAT score had the strongest negative pull, significantly increasing the predicted risk of failure since it fell well below what's typical for students who pass. Your undergraduate GPA followed closely, also nudging the prediction toward failure as it trailed the average for successful bar takers. Compared to typical students who pass, your academic metrics like LSAT and GPA align more with those who struggle on the first try, despite the positive signal from your later school decile. While full-time status is common and neutral here, these core academic indicators carried the day. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward or above the 36.70 average, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses if possible, and highlighting any upward trends like your Year 3 decile in appeals or re-applications. Many students in similar situations succeed by targeting these areas—your upper-middle background gives you resources to prep effectively. Keep pushing; law school doors reopen with targeted improvements.
